semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{l:semibranches} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree and $\Gamma\subseteq T$. Then, $\Gamma$ is a semibranch if and only if $\Gamma$ is a branch or $\Gamma=T_{\leq \gamma}$ where $\gamma=\max\Gamma$ and $\pi_\Gamma(a)=a\meet \gamma$ for all $a\in T$.
%\begin{proof} Suppose that $\Gamma$ is a semibranch which is not a branch. Then, there is some $a\in T\setminus \Gamma$ such that $\{a\}\cup \Gamma$ is linearly ordered. As $\Gamma$ is downwards closed, we conclude that $a>b$ for any $b\in\Gamma$. Then, $\gamma\coloneqq\pi_\Gamma(a)\geq b$ for all $b\in\Gamma$, i.e. $\gamma=\max\Gamma$. As $\Gamma$ is downwards closed, $\Gamma=T_{\leq\gamma}$. Also, for any $a\in T$, as $a\meet\gamma\leq a$ and $a\meet\gamma\in\Gamma$, $a\meet\gamma\leq \pi_\Gamma(a)$. On the other hand, for any $a\in T$, $\pi_\Gamma(a)\leq \gamma$ and $\pi_\Gamma(a)\leq a$, so $\pi_\Gamma(a)\leq a\meet \gamma$. Hence, we conclude that $\pi_\Gamma(a)=a\meet \gamma$ for any $a\in T$.
%
%Conversely, suppose that $\Gamma$ is a branch. Then, it is obviously downwards closed. On the other hand, take $a\in T$, so $a\in\Gamma$ or $a\notin\Gamma$. In the first case, $a=\max\{b\in\Gamma\tq b\leq a\}$. In the other case, as $\{a\}\cup \Gamma$ is not a chain, there is $c\in\Gamma$ such that $a\not >c$. Then, $a\meet c\in\Gamma$ and $a\meet c\leq a$. Take $b\in\Gamma$ arbitrary with $b\leq a$. As $\Gamma$ is linearly ordered, $b\leq a$ and $a\not>c$, we conclude that $b\leq c$, so $b\leq a\meet c$. Since $b$ is arbitrary, we get $a\meet c=\max\{b\in\Gamma\tq b\leq a\}$. As $a$ is arbitrary, we conclude that $\Gamma$ is a semibranch. 
%
%Finally, suppose $\Gamma=T_{\leq \gamma}$ with $\gamma\in T$. By definition, $\Gamma$ is a downwards closed chain and $\gamma=\max \Gamma$. On the other hand, for any $a\in T$, $a\meet\gamma=\max\{b\in T\tq b\leq \gamma\mathrm{\ and\ }b\leq a\}=\max\{b\in \Gamma\tq b\leq a\}$. Hence, $\Gamma$ is a semibranch. 
%\end{proof}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{c:semibranches tree} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree and $T^{\infty}\coloneqq\{\Gamma\subseteq T\tq \Gamma\mathrm{\ semibranch}\}$. Then, $(T^{\infty},\subseteq,\cap)$ is a meet{\hyp}tree and every element of $T^{\infty}$ is bounded by a maximal element, where the maximal elements of $T^{\infty}$ are the branches of $T$. Furthermore, the canonical map $\gamma\mapsto T_{\leq \gamma}$ is an embedding of meet{\hyp}trees from $T$ into $T^{\infty}$. In the particular case of $T^{\infty}$ in place of $T$, the canonical embedding of $T^{\infty}$ into $(T^{\infty})^{\infty}$ is an isomorphism.
%\begin{proof} Obviously, $T^{\infty}$ is a partially ordered set. We want to show that it is an ordered tree. Take $\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2,\Gamma\in T^{\infty}$ such that $\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2\subseteq \Gamma$. Suppose $\Gamma_1\not\subseteq\Gamma_2$. Then, there is $\gamma\in\Gamma_1\setminus \Gamma_2$. As $\Gamma$ is a chain and $\Gamma_2$ is downwards closed, we conclude that $\gamma>a$ for all $a\in \Gamma_2$. Since $\Gamma_1$ is also downwards closed, we conclude that $\Gamma_2\subseteq\Gamma_1$. As $\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2,\Gamma\in T^{\infty}$ are arbitrary, we conclude that $T^{\infty}$ is an ordered tree.
%
%Given $\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2\in T^{\infty}$, obviously $\Gamma_1\cap \Gamma_2$ is the largest subset of $T$ smaller than $\Gamma_1$ and $\Gamma_2$. Thus, it suffices to show that $\Gamma_1\cap\Gamma_2$ is a semibranch. Obviously, as subset of a chain, $\Gamma_1\cap\Gamma_2$ is a chain. Since $\Gamma_1$ and $\Gamma_2$ are downwards closed, $\Gamma_1\cap\Gamma_2$ is downwards closed as well. Take $a\in T$, we claim that $\pi_{\Gamma_1}(\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a))=\max\{b\in\Gamma_1\cap\Gamma_2\tq b\leq a\}$. Indeed, it is clear that $\pi_{\Gamma_1}(\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a))\leq a$ and, as $\Gamma_2$ is downwards closed, $\pi_{\Gamma_1}(\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a))\in\Gamma_1\cap \Gamma_2$. Take $b\in\Gamma_1\cap\Gamma_2$ arbitrary with $b\leq a$. As $b\in\Gamma_2$, we get $b\leq\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a)$. As $b\in\Gamma_1$, then $b\leq \pi_{\Gamma_1}(\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a))$. Since $b$ is arbitrary, we conclude that $\pi_{\Gamma_1}(\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a))=\max\{b\in\Gamma_1\cap\Gamma_2\tq b\leq a\}$. Hence, $\Gamma_1\cap \Gamma_2$ is a semibranch. As $\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2\in T^{\infty}$ are arbitrary, we conclude that $T^{\infty}$ is a meet{\hyp}tree.
%
%By definition and \cref{l:semibranches}, every branch of $T$ is a maximal element of $T^{\infty}$. On the other hand, if $\Gamma\subseteq T$ is a semibranch which is not a branch, there is $a\in T$ such that $a>b$ for all $b\in \Gamma$. Hence, $\Gamma\subset T_{\leq a}$, where $T_{\leq a}\in T^{\infty}$ by \cref{l:semibranches}, concluding that $\Gamma$ is not maximal in $T^{\infty}$. By Zorn's Lemma, any chain is contained in some branch so, in particular, every element of $T^{\infty}$ is bounded by some maximal element. 
%
%By \cref{l:semibranches}, $\inc:\ T\rightarrow T^{\infty}$ is well defined. Take $a,b\in T$. Obviously, $a\leq b$ if and only if $T_{\leq a}\subseteq T_{\leq b}$. On the other hand, $x\leq a$ and $x\leq b$ if and only if $x\leq a\meet b$. Thus, $T_{\leq a}\cap T_{\leq b}=T_{\leq a\meet b}$, concluding that $\inc$ is an embedding of meet{\hyp}trees. Finally, note that $(T^{\infty})^{\infty}\cong T^{\infty}$. Indeed, given any maximal chain $\{\Gamma_i\}_{i\in I}$ in $T^{\infty}$, $\bigcup \Gamma_i$ is a chain of $T$, so there is a branch $\Gamma\in T^{\infty}$ with $\Gamma_i\subseteq\Gamma$ for each $i\in I$. Hence, by maximality, $\{\Gamma_i\}_{i\in I}=\{\Gamma_i\in T^{\infty}\tq \Gamma_i\subseteq \Gamma\}$, concluding that $\inc:\ T^{\infty}\rightarrow (T^{\infty})^{\infty}$ is surjective (and so an isomorphism).
%\end{proof}

Since $b$ is arbitrary, we conclude that $\pi_{\Gamma_1}(\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a))=\max\{b\in\Gamma_1\cap\Gamma_2\tq b\leq a\}$. Hence, $\Gamma_1\cap \Gamma_2$ is a semibranch. As $\Gamma_1,\Gamma_2\in T^{\infty}$ are arbitrary, we conclude that $T^{\infty}$ is a meet{\hyp}tree.
%
%By definition and \cref{l:semibranches}, every branch of $T$ is a maximal element of $T^{\infty}$. On the other hand, if $\Gamma\subseteq T$ is a semibranch which is not a branch, there is $a\in T$ such that $a>b$ for all $b\in \Gamma$. Hence, $\Gamma\subset T_{\leq a}$, where $T_{\leq a}\in T^{\infty}$ by \cref{l:semibranches}, concluding that $\Gamma$ is not maximal in $T^{\infty}$. By Zorn's Lemma, any chain is contained in some branch so, in particular, every element of $T^{\infty}$ is bounded by some maximal element. 
%
%By \cref{l:semibranches}, $\inc:\ T\rightarrow T^{\infty}$ is well defined. Take $a,b\in T$. Obviously, $a\leq b$ if and only if $T_{\leq a}\subseteq T_{\leq b}$. On the other hand, $x\leq a$ and $x\leq b$ if and only if $x\leq a\meet b$. Thus, $T_{\leq a}\cap T_{\leq b}=T_{\leq a\meet b}$, concluding that $\inc$ is an embedding of meet{\hyp}trees. Finally, note that $(T^{\infty})^{\infty}\cong T^{\infty}$. Indeed, given any maximal chain $\{\Gamma_i\}_{i\in I}$ in $T^{\infty}$, $\bigcup \Gamma_i$ is a chain of $T$, so there is a branch $\Gamma\in T^{\infty}$ with $\Gamma_i\subseteq\Gamma$ for each $i\in I$. Hence, by maximality, $\{\Gamma_i\}_{i\in I}=\{\Gamma_i\in T^{\infty}\tq \Gamma_i\subseteq \Gamma\}$, concluding that $\inc:\ T^{\infty}\rightarrow (T^{\infty})^{\infty}$ is surjective (and so an isomorphism).
%\end{proof}

theorem l_generated (T : Type*) [MeetTree T] (Γ : Type*) [Semibranch Γ] (gamma : T) (X : Set T) :
    (⟨X⟩_gamma = ⟨X ∪ {gamma}⟩_∧) ∧
    (X.Finite → ∀ x ∈ X, ∀ y ∈ X, (x ⊓ y) = ⨅ z ∈ X, (x ⊓ z)) ∧
    (⟨X⟩_∧ = {x ⊓ y | x ∈ X ∧ y ∈ X}) ∧
    (X.Finite ∧ X.Nonempty → ∀ x ∉ X, ⟨X ∪ {x}⟩_∧ = ⟨X⟩_∧ ∪ {e, x} ∧
      (e := ⨆ y ∈ X, (x ⊓ y)) ∧ (card ⟨X⟩_∧ ≤ 2 * X.card - 1)) ∧
    (∀ x ∈ X, ∀ y ∈ X, pi_Γ (x ⊓ y) = pi_Γ x ⊓ pi_Γ y ∧ pi_Γ x ⊓ y = min (pi_Γ x) (pi_Γ y)) ∧
    (⟨X⟩_Γ = ⟨X⟩_∧ ∪ {pi_Γ x | x ∈ X} ∧ ⟨X⟩_Γ ∩ Γ = {pi_Γ x | x ∈ X}) ∧
    (X.Finite → ⟨X⟩_Γ = ⟨X ∪ {gamma_X}⟩_∧ ∧ gamma_X = ⨆ x ∈ X, pi_Γ x ∧ card ⟨X⟩_Γ ≤ 2 * X.card + 1 ∧
      ∀ gamma0 ∈ Γ, ⟨X ∪ {gamma0}⟩_∧ = ⟨X ∪ {gamma_X}⟩_∧ ∪ {gamma0}) ∧
    (cone_gamma X = cone_gamma ⟨X⟩_gamma ∧ cone_gamma ⟨X⟩_gamma = cone_gamma ⟨X⟩_Γ)

\label{l:generated} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\Gamma$ a semibranch, $\gamma$ a point and $X$ a subset. Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\arabic*)}}, ref={\rm{\arabic*}}, wide]
\item \label{itm:generated 1} $\langle X\rangle_{\gamma}=\langle X\,\gamma\rangle_{\wedge}$.
\item \label{itm:generated 2} If $X$ is finite and $x\in X$, $\bigwedge X=\min\{x\meet y\tq y\in X\}$. 
\item \label{itm:generated 3} $\langle X\rangle_{\wedge}=\{x\meet y\tq x,y\in X\}$.
\item \label{itm:generated 4} If $X$ is finite non{\hyp}empty and $x\notin X$,  
$\langle X\, x\rangle_{\wedge}=\langle X\rangle_{\wedge}\cup \{e,x\}$,
where $e\coloneqq\max\{x\meet y\tq y\in X\}$. It follows by induction that $|\langle X\rangle_{\wedge}|\leq 2|X|-1$.
\item \label{itm:generated 5} For any $x,y\in X$, we have $\pi_\Gamma(x\meet y)=\pi_\Gamma(x)\meet \pi_\Gamma(y)=\pi_\Gamma(x)\meet y=\min\{\pi_\Gamma(x),\pi_\Gamma(y)\}$.
\item \label{itm:generated 6} $\langle X\rangle_{\Gamma}=\langle X\rangle_{\wedge}\cup \{\pi_{\Gamma}(x)\tq x\in X\}$ and $\langle X\rangle_\Gamma\cap \Gamma=\{\pi_\Gamma(x)\tq x\in X\}$.
\item \label{itm:generated 7} If $X$ is finite, 
$\langle X\rangle_{\Gamma}=\langle X\,\gamma_X\rangle_{\wedge}$ where $\gamma_X=\max\{\pi_\Gamma(x)\tq x\in X\}$. By {\rm{(\cref{itm:generated 4})}}, $|\langle X\rangle_\Gamma|\leq 2|X|+1$. Moreover, for any $\gamma_0\in\Gamma$, we have 
$\langle X\,\gamma_0\rangle_{\wedge}=\langle X\,\gamma_X\rangle_{\wedge}\cup\{\gamma_0\}$.
\item \label{itm:generated 8} $\cone_\gamma(X)=\cone_\gamma(\langle X\rangle_\gamma)=\cone_\gamma(\langle X\rangle_\Gamma)$.
\end{enumerate}

Finally, for $|X|>2$, take $z\in X$ arbitrary. Then, we have that $\bigwedge X=\left(\bigwedge (X\setminus \{z\})\right)\meet z= \min\{x\meet y\tq y\in X\setminus\{z\}\}\meet z=\min\{x\meet y\meet z\tq y\in X\setminus\{z\}\}=\min\{\min\{x\meet z,x\meet y\}\tq y\in X\setminus\{z\}\}=\min\{x\meet y\tq y\in X\}$. 
%\item Trivially $\{x\meet y\tq x,y\in X\}\subseteq \langle X\rangle$. On the other hand, $\{x\meet y\tq x,y\in X\}$ contains $X$ and is closed under $\meet$ by the {\rm{(2)}}, so it is an $\lang_\wedge${\hyp}substructure, concluding that $\langle X\rangle\subseteq \{x\meet y\tq x,y\in X\}$ by minimality of $\langle X\rangle$. 
%\item When $X\subseteq T$ is finite, given $x\in X$, pick $e=\max\{x\meet y\tq y\in X\setminus\{x\}\}$, and say $e=x\meet y_0$. Then, for any $y\in X\setminus\{x\}$, we get that $x\meet y=x\meet y\meet e=x\meet y\meet y_0=\min\{y\meet y_0,e\}\in\langle X\setminus\{x\}\rangle\cup \{e\}$, concluding $\langle X\rangle=\langle X\setminus\{x\}\rangle\cup\{e,x\}$.  In particular, by induction on $|X|$, we get that $|\langle X\rangle|\leq 2|X|-1$. 
%\item We obviously have $\pi_\Gamma(x\meet y)\leq \pi_\Gamma(x)\meet \pi_\Gamma(y)\leq \min\{\pi_\Gamma(x),\pi_\Gamma(y)\}\leq \pi_\Gamma(x)\meet y$. Finally, note that $\pi_\Gamma(x)\meet y\leq \pi_\Gamma(x)$, so $\pi_\Gamma(x)\meet y\in\Gamma$. As $\pi_\Gamma(x)\meet y\leq x\meet y$, we conclude that $\pi_\Gamma(x)\meet y\leq \pi_\Gamma(x\meet y)$.
%
%In particular, we conclude that $\langle X\rangle_\Gamma=\langle X\rangle\cup \{\pi_\Gamma(x)\tq x\in X\}$. Indeed, trivially $\langle X\rangle \cup \{\pi_\Gamma(x)\tq x\in X\}\subseteq \langle X\rangle_\Gamma$. On the other hand, $\langle X\rangle\cup \{\pi_\Gamma(x)\tq x\in X\}$ contains $X$ and is closed under $\meet$ and $\pi_\Gamma$, so it is an $\lang_\Gamma${\hyp}substructure, concluding that $\langle X\rangle_\Gamma\subseteq \langle X\rangle\cup \{\pi_\Gamma(x)\tq x\in X\}$ by minimality of $\langle X\rangle_\Gamma$.
%
%Also, $\langle X\rangle_\Gamma\cap \Gamma=(\langle X\rangle\cap\Gamma)\cup \{\pi_{\Gamma}(x)\tq x\in X\}$. If $z\in \langle X\rangle\cap \Gamma$, then $z=x\meet y$ with $x,y\in X$ and $z=\pi_{\Gamma}(z)=\min\{\pi_{\Gamma}(x),\pi_{\Gamma}(y)\}$, so $\langle X\rangle_\Gamma\cap \Gamma=\{\pi_{\Gamma}(x)\tq x\in X\}$.
%

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem} \label{l:quantifier free type} Let $\lang$ be a language and $T$ a theory on $\lang$. Let $\lang_{\mathrm{rel}}$ be the relational reduct, i.e. the reduct consisting on the relation symbols of $\lang$. Assume that for every function symbol of $\lang$, there is a formula in $\lang_{\mathrm{rel}}$ defining it in $T$. Let $a$ and $b$ enumerate two models of $T$. Then, $\qftp_{\lang}(a)=\qftp_{\lang}(b)$ if and only if $\qftp_{\lang_{\mathrm{rel}}}(a)=\qftp_{\lang_{\mathrm{rel}}}(b)$. In other words, every $\lang_{\mathrm{rel}}${\hyp}isomorphism between two models of $T$ is an $\lang${\hyp}isomorphism. \begin{proof} Note that, by the hypotheses, any $\lang_{\mathrm{rel}}${\hyp}isomorphism of $T${\hyp}models is an $\lang${\hyp}isomorphism. \end{proof} \end{lem}

Let $a$ and $b$ enumerate two models of $T$.

theorem c_quantifier_free_type_tree {T : Type*} {Γ : Set T} {gamma : T} {n : ℕ} (a b : Fin n → T)
    (hT : IsMeetTree T) (hΓ : IsSemibranch Γ) (ha : ∀ i, a i ∈ T) (hb : ∀ i, b i ∈ T) :
    (qftp_gamma a = qftp_gamma b ↔ qftp_∧ (a, gamma) = qftp_∧ (b, gamma)) ∧
    (qftp_∧ a = qftp_∧ b ↔ (∀ i j k, a i ⊓ a j ≤ a k ↔ b i ⊓ b j ≤ b k)) ∧
    (let gamma_a := sSup {pi_Γ (a i) | i : Fin n};
     let gamma_b := sSup {pi_Γ (b i) | i : Fin n};
     qftp_Γ a = qftp_Γ b ↔ qftp_∧ (a, gamma_a) = qftp_∧ (b, gamma_b) ∧
     (∀ gamma' ∈ Γ, gamma' > gamma_a → gamma' > gamma_b → qftp_Γ a = qftp_Γ b ↔ qftp_gamma' a = qftp_gamma' b))

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{c:quantifier free type tree}
Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\Gamma$ a semibranch and $\gamma$ a point. Let $a=(a_i)_{i<n}$ and $b=(b_i)_{i<n}$ be finite tuples in $T$. Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\arabic*)}}, ref={\rm{\arabic*}}, wide]
\item \label{itm:quantifier free type tree 1} $\qftp_{\gamma}(a)=\qftp_{\gamma}(b)$ if and only if $\qftp_{\wedge}(a,\gamma)=\qftp_{\wedge}(b,\gamma)$.
\item \label{itm:quantifier free type tree 2} $\qftp_{\wedge}(a)=\qftp_{\wedge}(b)$ if and only if, for any $i,j,k<n$, we have that $a_i\meet a_j\leq a_k$ if and only if $b_i\meet b_j\leq b_k$.
\item \label{itm:quantifier free type tree 3} Pick $\gamma_a=\max\{\pi_{\Gamma}(a_i)\}_{i<n}$ and $\gamma_b=\max\{\pi_{\Gamma}(b_i)\}_{i<n}$. Then, $\qftp_{\Gamma}(a)=\qftp_{\Gamma}(b)$ if and only if $\qftp_{\wedge}(a,\gamma_a)=\qftp_{\wedge}(b,\gamma_b)$. Furthermore, for any $\gamma\in \Gamma$ with $\gamma>\gamma_a$ and $\gamma>\gamma_b$, $\qftp_{\Gamma}(a)=\qftp_{\Gamma}(b)$ if and only if $\qftp_\gamma(a)=\qftp_\gamma(b)$.
\end{enumerate}
%\begin{proof} {\rm{(1)}} Trivial.\smallskip

%{\rm{(2)}} The forward direction is obvious. For the backward direction, consider the map $f:\ \langle a\rangle\rightarrow\langle b\rangle$ given by $f(a_i\meet a_j)=b_i\meet b_j$. By our assumption, note that $a_i\meet a_j=a_k\meet a_s$ if and only if $b_i\meet b_j=b_k\meet b_s$. Thus, $f$ is an injective partial function. By \cref{l:generated}(2), $f$ is defined in the whole set $\langle a\rangle$ and surjective. Finally, by assumption, $f$ is order preserving, so an isomorphism of ordered trees. Thus, $f$ is an isomorphism of meet{\hyp}trees. In particular, $\qftp(a)=\qftp(b)$.\smallskip

%{\rm{(3)}} The forward direction is obvious. For the backward direction, suppose $\qftp(a,\gamma_a)=\qftp(b,\gamma_b)$. Then, $a_i\mapsto b_i$ and $\gamma_a\mapsto \gamma_b$ extends uniquely to an isomorphism $f:\ \langle a,\gamma_a\rangle\rightarrow \langle b,\gamma_b\rangle$ of meet{\hyp}trees. By \cref{l:generated}(3), $\langle a,\gamma_a\rangle=\langle a\rangle_{\Gamma}$ and $\langle b,\gamma_b\rangle=\langle b\rangle_\Gamma$. Now, for any $x\in\langle a,\gamma_a\rangle$, $f(\pi_{\Gamma}(x))=f(x\meet\gamma_a)=f(x)\meet \gamma_b=\pi_{\Gamma}(f(x))$, so $f$ is actually a isomorphism between the semibranched meet{\hyp}trees $\langle a\rangle_\Gamma$ and $\langle b\rangle_\Gamma$. Hence, $\qftp_{\Gamma}(a)=\qftp_{\Gamma}(b)$.

%For $\gamma\in\Gamma$ with $\gamma>\gamma_a$ and $\gamma>\gamma_b$, by \cref{l:generated}(3), we have that $\langle a,\gamma\rangle=\langle a,\gamma_a\rangle\cup\{\gamma\}$ and $\langle b,\gamma\rangle=\langle a,\gamma_b\rangle\cup\{\gamma\}$. Hence, if $\qftp(a,\gamma)=\qftp(b,\gamma)$, in particular we get that $\qftp(a,\gamma_a)=\qftp(b,\gamma_b)$. Conversely, if $\qftp(a,\gamma_a)=\qftp(b,\gamma_b)$, then $a_i\mapsto b_i$ and $\gamma_a\mapsto \gamma_b$ extends uniquely to an isomorphism of meet{\hyp}trees $f:\ \langle a,\gamma_a\rangle\rightarrow \langle b,\gamma_b\rangle$. Now, consider the extension $f':\ \langle a,\gamma\rangle\rightarrow \langle b,\gamma\rangle$ given by mapping $\gamma\mapsto \gamma$. For $x\in\langle a,\gamma_a\rangle$, if $x\leq \gamma$, then $x\in\Gamma$, so $x\leq \gamma_a$. As $f$ is an isomorphism of meet{\hyp}trees, we conclude $f'(x)=f(x)\leq \gamma_b\leq \gamma=f'(\gamma)$. Symmetrically, if $f(x)=f'(x)\leq f'(\gamma)=\gamma$, we get that $x\leq \gamma$. Hence, $f'$ is also an isomorphism of meet{\hyp}trees, so $\qftp(a,\gamma)=\qftp(b,\gamma)$. 
%\end{proof}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{coro} \label{c:cones are indiscernible} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\gamma$ a point, $C$ a finite subset of $T$ and $a,b$ single elements above $\gamma$. Suppose $a\notin\cone_{\gamma}(C)$ and $b\notin \cone_\gamma(C)$. Then, $\qftp_\gamma(a/C)=\qftp_\gamma(b/C)$. \begin{proof} By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 4}), we have that $\langle a,C\rangle_\gamma=\langle C\rangle_\gamma\cup \{a\}$ and $\langle b,C\rangle_\gamma=\langle C\rangle_\gamma\cup\{b\}$. Furthermore, for $x\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$, $x\leq a$ if and only if $x\leq \gamma$, if and only if $x\leq b$. Thus, by \cref{l:quantifier free type}, we conclude that $\qftp_\gamma(a/C)=\qftp_\gamma(b/C)$. \end{proof} \end{coro}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{coro}\label{c:semibranch intervals are indiscernible} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\Gamma$ a semibranch, $C$ a finite subset of $T$ and $a,b$ single elements in $\Gamma$. Write $\pi(C)=\{\pi_{\Gamma}(c)\tq c\in C\}$. Then, $\qftp_\Gamma(a/C)=\qftp_\Gamma(b/C)$ if and only if $\qftp_{<}(a/\pi(C))=\qftp_{<}(b/\pi(C))$. \begin{proof} The ``only if'' implication is obvious, let's prove the ``if'' one. Set $\bar{C}=\langle C\rangle_{\Gamma}$. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 7}), $\langle a\, C\rangle_\Gamma=\bar{C}\cup \{a\}$ and $\langle b\, C\rangle_\Gamma=\bar{C}\cup \{b\}$. By \cref{l:quantifier free type}, $\qftp_\Gamma(b/C)=\qftp_\Gamma(a/C)$ if and only if $\qftp_{<}(b/\bar{C})=\qftp_{<}(a/\bar{C})$. Pick $c\in\bar{C}$ arbitrary. If $c\leq a$, then $c\in \Gamma$ so, by \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 6}), $c\in\pi(C)$ and, by hypothesis, $c\leq b$. If $a\leq c$, as $a\in \Gamma$, then $a\leq \pi_{\Gamma}(c)\leq c$ and, by \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 6}), $\pi_{\Gamma}(c)\in \pi(C)$, so $b\leq \pi_{\Gamma}(c)\leq c$ by hypothesis. Symmetrically, $c\leq b$ implies $c\leq a$ and $b\leq c$ implies $a\leq c$. \end{proof} \end{coro}

[Three points]\label{l:three elements} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree and $a,b,c$ three points. At least two of the three possible pairwise meets between $a,b,c$ equal $a\meet b\meet c$. In other words, if $a\meet b
eq a\meet b\meet c$, then $a\meet c=b\meet c=a\meet b\meet c$.    
 egin{proof} See \cite[Fact 2.19]{kaplan2020automorphism}.% Assume $a\meet b
eq a\meet b\meet c$. As $\max\{a\meet c,b\meet c\}\leq c$, we conclude that $a\meet b
ot\leq \max\{a\meet c,b\meet c\}$. Since $a\meet b,a\meet c\leq a$ and $a\meet b,b\meet c\leq b$, we get that $a\meet b>\max\{a\meet c,b\meet c\}$. Hence, $\min\{a\meet c,b\meet c\}\geq a\meet b\meet c\geq \max\{a\meet c,b\meet c\}$, concluding that $a\meet c=b\meet c$. 
\end{proof}

Conclusion: If $a\meet c=b\meet c$ and $a\meet d\neq b\meet d$, then $a\meet c=d\meet c$. \begin{proof} By \cref{l:three elements} with $a,b,d$, either $a\meet b=a\meet d<b\meet d$ or $a\meet b= b\meet d<a\meet d$. By \cref{l:three elements} with $a,b,c$, we have $a\meet c=b\meet c\leq a\meet b$. Therefore, $a\meet c<a\meet d$, concluding $a\meet c=d\meet c$ by \cref{l:three elements} with $a,c,d$. \end{proof}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem}[Four points]\label{l:four elements} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree and $a,b,c,d$ four points. If $a\meet c=b\meet c$ and $a\meet d\neq b\meet d$, then $a\meet c=d\meet c$. \begin{proof} By \cref{l:three elements} with $a,b,d$, either $a\meet b=a\meet d<b\meet d$ or $a\meet b= b\meet d<a\meet d$. Since the statement is symmetric on $a$ and $b$, we can assume without loss of generality that $b\meet d<a\meet d$. By \cref{l:three elements} with $a,b,c$, we have $a\meet c=b\meet c\leq a\meet b$. Therefore, $a\meet c<a\meet d$, concluding $a\meet c=d\meet c$ by \cref{l:three elements} with $a,c,d$. \end{proof} \end{lem}

entries.lean_statement:
lemma l_fraisse_trees
    {V : Type*} [MeasurableSpace V] [Fintype V]
    (T : V → V → Prop)
    (Γ : V → V → Prop)
    (h_meet : ∀ x y, T x y ∨ T y x)
    (h_semibranch : ∀ x y, Γ x y → T x y)
    (h_dense : ∀ x y, T x y → ∃ z, Γ z x ∧ Γ z y)
    (h_ulb : ∀ n : ℕ, ∃ m : ℕ, ∀ T', Fintype.card T' ≤ m) :
    ∃ (L : Type*) [MeasurableSpace L] [Fintype L],
    (∀ x y : L, T x y ∨ T y x) ∧
    (∀ x y : L, Γ x y → T x y) ∧
    (∀ x y : L, T x y → ∃ z, Γ z x ∧ Γ z y) ∧
    (∀ n : ℕ, ∃ m : ℕ, ∀ T', Fintype.card T' ≤ m)

assumptions.label: h_meet
assumptions.lean_expr: (h_meet : ∀ x y, T x y ∨ T y x)

assumptions.label: h_semibranch
assumptions.lean_expr: (h_semibranch : ∀ x y, Γ x y → T x y)

assumptions.label: h_dense
assumptions.lean_expr: (h_dense : ∀ x y, T x y → ∃ z, Γ z x ∧ Γ z y)

assumptions.label: h_ulb
assumptions.lean_expr: (h_ulb : ∀ n : ℕ, ∃ m : ℕ, ∀ T', Fintype.card T' ≤ m)

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{l:fraisse trees} 
%\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\arabic*)}}, ref={\rm{\arabic*}}]
%\item[{\hspace{-1.8em}} \rm{(1)}] \setcounter{enumi}{1} \label{itm:fraisse trees 1} The class of pointed meet{\hyp}trees is a uniformly locally finite strong Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} class in the language of pointed meet{\hyp}trees, and its Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} limit is (isomorphic to) the universal dense pointed meet{\hyp}tree. In particular, the universal dense pointed meet{\hyp}tree is $\omega${\hyp}categorical, homogeneous and has quantifier elimination in the language of pointed meet{\hyp}trees.
%\item \label{itm:fraisse trees 2} 
The class of finite pointed/semibranched meet{\hyp}trees is a uniformly locally finite strong Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} class in the language of pointed/semibranched meet{\hyp}trees. Moreover, its Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} limit is (isomorphic to) the branched/pointed universal dense meet{\hyp}tree. In particular, these structures are $\omega${\hyp}categorical, homogeneous and have quantifier elimination in their corresponding languages.
%\end{enumerate}
\begin{proof} The fact that these classes are strong Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} classes is straightforward. The ``moreover'' part is a particular case of \cref{l:generic meet-tree expansions} below.
%
%{\rm{(1)}} Note that any two (single) elements in $\Tt$ satisfy the same quantifier free type. Thus, the conclusion is trivial by homogeneity of $\Tt$, as we have only added a constant.\smallskip
%
%{\rm{(2)}} Let $\Gamma\subseteq \Tt$ be a branch; we want to show that $\Tt_\Gamma$ is the Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} limit of finite semibranched meet{\hyp}trees. Let $T_2$ be a finite semibranched meet{\hyp}tree and $T_1$ a substructure of $T_2$ as semibranched meet{\hyp}tree. Write $\Gamma_2$ and $\Gamma_1$ for the respective semibranches of $T_2$ and $T_1$. Let $f:\ T_1\rightarrow \Tt_\Gamma$ be an embedding of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees. We want to show that there is an embedding $f':\ T_2\rightarrow \Tt_\Gamma$ of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees extending $f$. 
%
%Let $(T_2',\Gamma_2')$ be an extension of $(T_2,\Gamma_2)$ given by adding an element $\gamma$ to the semibranch maximal among all the elements of the tree. Note that, then, $\pi_{\Gamma_2'}(a)=a\meet \gamma$ for any element $a\in T_2'$ by \cref{l:generated}(3). We are going to extend $f$ to an embedding of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees $f':\ T_2'\rightarrow \Tt_\Gamma$. 
%
%Consider the subtree $T_1'=T_1\cup \Gamma_2'$ of $T'_2$, which is a semibranched meet{\hyp}tree with semibranch $\Gamma'_2$. Note that $\Gamma$ is a dense linear order (without end points), so, by the universal property of dense linear orders, there is an embedding of ordered sets $h_0:\ \Gamma_2'\rightarrow \Gamma$ extending $f_{\mid \Gamma_1}$. 
%
%Now, consider $h=f\cup h_0:\ T'_1\rightarrow \Tt_\Gamma$. Note that $h$ is an embedding of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees. Indeed, it is obviously a well defined map. By construction, for any $a\in T'_1$,  $a\in\Gamma'_2$ if and only if $h(a)\in \Gamma$. Now, take $a,b\in T'_1$; we want to show that $a\leq b$ if and only if $h(a)\leq h(b)$. Either $a,b\in T_1$, $a,b\in \Gamma_2'$, $a\in T_1$ and $b\in\Gamma'_2$ or $a\in \Gamma'_2$ and $b\in T_1$. As $f$ and $h_0$ are embeddings, the first two cases follow trivially. The third case reduces to the second case as $\Gamma_2'$ and $\Gamma$ are downwards closed. For the latter case, we have that $a\leq b$ implies  $a\leq \pi_{\Gamma_1}(b)\leq b$ and $h(a)\leq h(b)$ implies $h(a)\leq \pi_{\Gamma}(h(b))\leq h(b)$ where $\pi_{\Gamma}(h(b))=h(\pi_{\Gamma_1}(b))$.  Thus, $a\leq b$ if and only if $h(a)\leq h(b)$ by the first two cases. 
%
%By the universal property of $\Tt$, there is an embedding of meet{\hyp}trees $f':\ T'_2\rightarrow \Tt$ extending $h:\ T'_1\rightarrow \Tt$. Since the semibranch of $T'_2$ is exactly the semibranch of $T'_1$, we have that $f'$ preserves the semibranch. On the other hand, for any $a\in T'_2$, as $f'(\gamma)\in \Gamma$ and $f'(a)\not\geq f'(\gamma)$, we get that $\pi_{\Gamma}(f'(a))=f'(a)\meet f'(\gamma)=f'(a\meet \gamma)=f'(\pi_{\Gamma'_2}(a))$. Thus, $f'$ is an embedding of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees.
%
%In particular, taking $T_1$ the one{\hyp}point semibranched meet{\hyp}tree, we get that the class of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees is the age of the countable universal dense branched meet{\hyp}tree. Since it is countable and satisfies the universal property of the Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} limit, we conclude that the universal dense branched meet{\hyp}tree is the Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} limit of the class of finite semibranched meet{\hyp}trees and that this class is a Fra\"{\i}ss\'{e} class. Finally, by \cref{l:generated}(3), a semibranched meet{\hyp}tree generated by $n$ elements has at most $2n+1$ elements. Thus, the class of finite semibranched meet{\hyp}trees is uniformly locally finite, getting quantifier elimination in the language of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees.
%
%It remains to show that, actually, the class of finite semibranched meet{\hyp}trees has the strong amalgamation property and the strong joint property. Let $(T_1,\Gamma_1)$, $(T_2,\Gamma_2)$ and $(T_3,\Gamma_3)$ be finite semibranched meet{\hyp}trees with $T_3\subseteq T_1$ and $T_3\subseteq T_2$ --- and allow $T_3=\emptyset$ for the strong joint property. Consider the meet{\hyp}trees $T'_1$ and $T'_2$ extending $T_1$ and $T_2$ given by adding a common maximal element $m$ with $m> a$ if and only if $a\in \Gamma_1$ for $a\in T_1$ and $m>a$ if and only if $a\in \Gamma_2$ for $a\in T_2$ --- note that, then, $m\meet a=\pi_{\Gamma_1}(a)$ for any $a\in T_1$ and $m\meet a=\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a)$ for any $a\in T_2$ by \cref{l:generated}(3). Now, consider $T_3'=T_3\cup \{m\}$. Note that $T_3'$ is a common substructure of $T_1'$ and $T'_2$ as meet{\hyp}tree --- indeed, for any $a\in T_3$, $a<m$ if and only if $a\in \Gamma_3$ and $a\meet m=\pi_{\Gamma_3}(a)\in T_3$. As the class of finite meet{\hyp}trees has the strong amalgamation property, there is a meet{\hyp}tree $T$ and embeddings of meet{\hyp}trees $f:\ T'_1\rightarrow T$ and $g:\ T'_2\rightarrow T$ such that $f_{\mid T'_3}=g_{\mid T'_3}$ and $f(T'_1)\cap g(T'_2)=f(T'_3)$. Take the semibranch of $T$ given by $\Gamma=\{a\tq a<f(m)=g(m)\}$. Then, $\pi_{\Gamma}(f(a))=f(a)\meet f(m)=f(a\meet m)=f(\pi_{\Gamma_1}(a))$ for any $a\in T_1$ and $\pi_{\Gamma}(g(a))=g(a)\meet g(m)=g(a\meet m)=g(\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a))$ for any $a\in T_2$, concluding that $f:\ (T_1,\Gamma_1)\rightarrow (T,\Gamma)$ and $g:\ (T_2,\Gamma_2)\rightarrow (T,\Gamma)$ are embeddings of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees with $f_{\mid T_3}=g_{\mid T_3}$ and $f(T_1)\cap g(T_2)=f(T_3)$.
\end{proof}

    {V : Type*} [MeasurableSpace V] [Fintype V]
    (T : V → V → Prop)
    (Γ : V → V → Prop)
    (h_meet : ∀ x y, T x y ∨ T y x)
    (h_semibranch : ∀ x y, Γ x y → T x y)
    (h_dense : ∀ x y, T x y → ∃ z, Γ z x ∧ Γ z y)
    (h_ulb : ∀ n : ℕ, ∃ m : ℕ, ∀ T', Fintype.card T' ≤ m) :
    ∃ (L : Type*) [MeasurableSpace L] [Fintype L],
    (∀ x y : L, T x y ∨ T y x) ∧
    (∀ x y : L, Γ x y → T x y) ∧
    (∀ x y : L, T x y → ∃ z, Γ z x ∧ Γ z y) ∧
    (∀ n : ℕ, ∃ m : ℕ, ∀ T', Fintype.card T' ≤ m)

%It remains to show that, actually, the class of finite semibranched meet{\hyp}trees has the strong amalgamation property and the strong joint property. Let $(T_1,\Gamma_1)$, $(T_2,\Gamma_2)$ and $(T_3,\Gamma_3)$ be finite semibranched meet{\hyp}trees with $T_3\subseteq T_1$ and $T_3\subseteq T_2$ --- and allow $T_3=\emptyset$ for the strong joint property. Consider the meet{\hyp}trees $T'_1$ and $T'_2$ extending $T_1$ and $T_2$ given by adding a common maximal element $m$ with $m> a$ if and only if $a\in \Gamma_1$ for $a\in T_1$ and $m>a$ if and only if $a\in \Gamma_2$ for $a\in T_2$ --- note that, then, $m\meet a=\pi_{\Gamma_1}(a)$ for any $a\in T_1$ and $m\meet a=\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a)$ for any $a\in T_2$ by \cref{l:generated}(3). Now, consider $T_3'=T_3\cup \{m\}$. Note that $T_3'$ is a common substructure of $T_1'$ and $T'_2$ as meet{\hyp}tree --- indeed, for any $a\in T_3$, $a<m$ if and only if $a\in \Gamma_3$ and $a\meet m=\pi_{\Gamma_3}(a)\in T_3$. As the class of finite meet{\hyp}trees has the strong amalgamation property, there is a meet{\hyp}tree $T$ and embeddings of meet{\hyp}trees $f:\ T'_1\rightarrow T$ and $g:\ T'_2\rightarrow T$ such that $f_{\mid T'_3}=g_{\mid T'_3}$ and $f(T'_1)\cap g(T'_2)=f(T'_3)$. Take the semibranch of $T$ given by $\Gamma=\{a\tq a<f(m)=g(m)\}$. Then, $\pi_{\Gamma}(f(a))=f(a)\meet f(m)=f(a\meet m)=f(\pi_{\Gamma_1}(a))$ for any $a\in T_1$ and $\pi_{\Gamma}(g(a))=g(a)\meet g(m)=g(a\meet m)=g(\pi_{\Gamma_2}(a))$ for any $a\in T_2$, concluding that $f:\ (T_1,\Gamma_1)\rightarrow (T,\Gamma)$ and $g:\ (T_2,\Gamma_2)\rightarrow (T,\Gamma)$ are embeddings of semibranched meet{\hyp}trees with $f_{\mid T_3}=g_{\mid T_3}$ and $f(T_1)\cap g(T_2)=f(T_3)$.
\end{proof}

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem}\label{l:cones and cone independence} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\gamma$ a point and $A,B,C$ subsets of $T$ such that $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. Then, $\cone_\gamma(AC)\cap \cone_\gamma(BC)=\cone_\gamma(C)$. \begin{proof} It is enough to show that if $a\in A$ and $b\in B$ such that $a\in\cone_\gamma(b)$, then $a\in\cone_\gamma(C)$. As $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, either $a\meet b\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$ or there is $c\in \langle C\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet c\neq b\meet c$. In the first case, $a\meet b\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$ and $a\in\cone_\gamma(a\meet b)$. In the second case, by \cref{l:three elements}, $\gamma<a\meet b=a\meet b\meet c\leq a\meet c$, so $a\in\cone_\gamma(c)$. \end{proof} \end{lem}
definitions: Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\gamma$ a point and $A,B,C$ subsets of $T$ such that $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$.
local_assumptions: if $a\in A$ and $b\in B$ such that $a\in\cone_\gamma(b)$, then $a\in\cone_\gamma(C)$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{l:cone-independence and generated} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree and $\gamma$ a point. Then, $\indepe[\gamma]{}$ has the \emph{independent generation property}: 
\[A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B\Rightarrow\langle ABC\rangle_\gamma=\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cup\langle BC\rangle_\gamma.\]
\begin{proof} Without loss of generality, we can assume that $A=\langle AC\rangle_{\gamma}$, $B=\langle BC\rangle_{\gamma}$ and $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. We want to show that $\langle AB\rangle_\wedge=A\cup B$. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we only need to check that $a\meet b\in A\cup B$ for any $a\in A$ and $b\in B$. As $A\indepe[\gamma]{C} B$, either $a\meet b\in C\subseteq A\cup B$, or there is $c\in C$ such that $a\meet c\neq b\meet c$. Now, in the latter case, by \cref{l:three elements}, $a\meet b=a\meet b\meet c=\min\{a\meet c,b\meet c\}\in A\cup B$.
\end{proof}

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{l:cone-independence finite} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree and $\gamma$ a point. Then, $\indepe[\gamma]{}$ satisfies the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}[wide]
\item[\rm{(Quantifier free definability)}] For any finite variables $x,y,z$, there is a quantifier free formula $\phi(x,y,z)$ in $\lang_\gamma$ such that, for any tuples $a,b,c$ (on the corresponding variables), $a\indepe[\gamma]{c}b$ if and only if $\vDash \phi(a,b,c)$.
\item[\rm{(Quantifier free invariance)}] If $a\indepe[\gamma]{c}b$, then $a'\indepe[\gamma]{c'}b'$  for any $a',b',c'$ such that $\qftp_{\gamma}(a',b',c')=\qftp_{\gamma}(a,b,c)$. 
\item[\rm{(Embedding invariance)}] If $f:\ T\rightarrow T'$ is an embedding of meet{\hyp}trees, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ if and only if $f(A)\indepe[f(\gamma)]{f(C)}f(B)$.
\item[\rm{(Monotonicity)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $A_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0\subseteq B$, then $A_0\indepe[\gamma]{C}B_0$.
\item[\rm{(Normality)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, then $AC\indepe[\gamma]{C}BC$.
\item[\rm{(Left and right existence)}] $A\indepe[\gamma]{A}B$ and $A\indepe[\gamma]{B}B$.
\item[\rm{(Finite character)}] $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ if and only if $A_0\indepe[\gamma]{C}B_0$ for every $A_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0\subseteq B$ finite.
\item[\rm{(Left and right base monotonicity)}] Suppose $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $C\subseteq D\subseteq A$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$. If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$.
\item[\rm{(Left and right transitivity)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$ and $D\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ with $C\subseteq D\subseteq A$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. If $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$ and $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$ with $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$.
\item[\rm{(Quantifier free stationarity)}] Suppose $a\indepe[\gamma]{C}b$ and $a'\indepe[\gamma]{C}b'$ with $\qftp_{\gamma}(a'/C)$ $=\qftp_{\gamma}(a/C)$ and $\qftp_{\gamma}(b'/C)=\qftp_{\gamma}(b/C)$, then $\qftp_{\gamma}(a'b'/C)=\qftp_{\gamma}(ab/C)$.
\end{enumerate}
\begin{proof} Quantifier free definability is obvious from \cref{d:cone-independence}. Quantifier free invariance and embedding invariance are trivial consequences of quantifier free definability. Monotonicity, normality, existence and finite character are trivial. It remains to check base monotonicity, transitivity and quantifier free stationarity:
\begin{enumerate}[wide]
\item[\rm{(Base monotonicity)}] Let $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$. We want to show that $A\indepe[\gamma]{C'}B$. Note that, by monotonicity, $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$. By  \cref{l:cone-independence and generated}, $\langle AD\rangle_\gamma= \langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cup \langle D\rangle_\gamma$. Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\roman*)}}, wide]
%\item $\langle A,C'\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle B,C'\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle C\rangle_{\gamma}\cup\langle C'\rangle_\gamma=\langle C'\rangle_\gamma$. 
\item Pick $a\in\langle AD\rangle_\gamma=\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cup\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle B\rangle_\gamma$ such that $b\leq a$. If $a\in \langle D\rangle_\gamma$, then $b\leq a\leq a$ with $a\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$. Otherwise, $a\in\langle AC\rangle_\gamma$ and, as $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, there is $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma\subseteq\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ such that $b\leq c\leq a$.
\item Pick $a\in\langle AD\rangle_\gamma=\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cup\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle B\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet b\notin \langle D\rangle_\gamma$. If $a\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$, we get that $a\meet a\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ and $a\meet b\notin \langle D\rangle_\gamma$, so $a\meet a\neq a\meet b$ with $a\in \langle D\rangle_\gamma$. Otherwise, $a\in\langle AC\rangle_\gamma$ and, as $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, there is $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma\subseteq\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet c\neq b\meet c$.
\end{enumerate}

This proves right base monotonicity, and left base monotonicity follows by the symmetric argument.
\item[\rm{(Transitivity)}] Let $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$ and $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$ with $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$. We want to show that $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. 
\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\roman*)}}, wide]
%\item $\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle BC\rangle_\gamma=\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle AD\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle B\rangle_\gamma\subseteq\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle D\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle C\rangle_\gamma$.
\item Pick $a\in\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle AD\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle BD\rangle_\gamma$ with $b\leq a$. As $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$, there is $d\in \langle D\rangle_\gamma$ such that $b\leq d\leq a$. Now, since $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$, there is $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$ such that $d\leq c\leq a$, concluding $b\leq c\leq a$.
\item Pick $a\in\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle AD\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle BD\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet c= b\meet c$ for all $c\in \langle C\rangle_\gamma$. Either $a\meet d=b\meet d$ for all $d\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ or there is $d\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet d\neq b\meet d$. 

In the first case, as $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$, we conclude $a\meet b\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$. Now, for any $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$, as $a\meet c=b\meet c$, using \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 2}), we get that $a\meet c=(a\meet c)\meet (b\meet c)=(a\meet b)\meet c$. As $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$, we conclude that $a\meet b=a\meet (a\meet b)\in \langle C\rangle_\gamma$.

In the second case, by \cref{l:four elements}, $a\meet c=d\meet c$ for any $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. Therefore, as $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$, we conclude that $a\meet d\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. In particular, $a\meet d=a\meet (a\meet d)=b\meet (a\meet d)=a\meet b\meet d$. Since $a\meet d\neq b\meet d$, by \cref{l:three elements}, we conclude that $a\meet b=a\meet b\meet d=a\meet d\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$.
\end{enumerate}

This proves right transitivity, and left transitivity follows by the symmetric argument.
\item[\rm{(Quantifier free stationarity)}] By embedding invariance it suffices to consider the case of the universal dense meet{\hyp}tree. By quantifier free invariance, it is enough to show that if $a \indepe[\gamma]{C} B$ and $a'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ with $\qftp(a/C)= \qftp(a'/C)$, then $\qftp_\gamma(a/CB)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/CB)$. It is enough to do the case where $|a|=1$ by monotonicity, base monotonicity and invariance. %Indeed, prove the statement by induction on $|a|$: say $a=a_{<n}a_n$ where $|a_n|=1$ and $n>0$, then, by induction hypothesis and monotonicity, $\qftp(a_{<n}/Cb)=\qftp(a_{<n}'/Cb)$ and, by invariance, we may assume $a_0=a_0'$; but then $a_1'\indepe[\gamma]{Ca_0}b$ by base monotonicity and, by induction hypothesis, $\qftp(a_1/Ca_0b)=\qftp(a_1'/Ca_0b)$.

Without loss of generality, say $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$ and $B=\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. There are two cases: there is $c\in C$ with $a\leq c$ or $e\coloneqq \max\{a\meet c\tq c\in C\}<a$. 

In the first case, take $c\in C$ with $a\leq c$. Since $\qftp(a/C)=\qftp(a'/C)$, $a'\leq c$. By \cref{l:no based cone-independence definition}, $a\indepe[c]{C}B$ and $a'\indepe[c]{C}B$. By point \ref{itm:def cone-independence i} of \cref{d:cone-independence}, $\qftp_{<}(a/\pi(B))=\qftp_{<}(a'/\pi(B))$ where $\pi(B)=\{b\meet c\tq b\in B\}=\{b\in B\tq b\leq c\}$. Then, by \cref{c:semibranch intervals are indiscernible}, $\qftp_\Gamma(a/B)=\qftp_\Gamma(a'/B)$ where $\Gamma$ is the semibranch given by $c$. By \cref{c:quantifier free type tree}(\ref{itm:quantifier free type tree 1},\ref{itm:quantifier free type tree 3}), $\qftp_\gamma(a/B)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/B)$.

In the second case, let $e\coloneqq\max\{a\meet c\tq c\in C\}$ and $e'\coloneqq\max\{a'\meet c\tq c\in C\}$. Since $\qftp_\gamma(a/C)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/C)$, we have $\qftp_\gamma(e/C)=\qftp_\gamma(e'/C)$. Also, by monotonicity, we have $e\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $e'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, but now there is $c\in C$ with $e\leq c$. Thus, by the previous case, $\qftp_\gamma(e/B)=\qftp_\gamma(e'/B)$. By invariance, we may assume $e=e'$. By base monotonicity, $a\indepe[\gamma]{Ce}B$ and $a'\indepe[\gamma]{Ce}B$ with $\qftp_\gamma(a/Ce)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/Ce)$. By \cref{l:no based cone-independence definition}, $a\indepe[e]{Ce}B$ and $a'\indepe[e]{Ce}B$. By choice of $e$, $a,a'\notin \cone_e(C)$. By \cref{l:cones and cone independence}, $a,a'\notin\cone_e(B)$. Hence, by \cref{c:cones are indiscernible}, $\qftp_e(a/B)=\qftp_e(a'/B)$. By \cref{c:quantifier free type tree}(\ref{itm:quantifier free type tree 1}), $\qftp_\gamma(a/B)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/B)$.
%
%Say $A=\{a_i\}_{i<n}$ and $A'=\{a'_i\}_{i<n}$. Let $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $A'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ with $\qftp_{\gamma}(a/C)=\qftp_{\gamma}(a'/C)$. Without loss of generality, assume $A=\langle A,C\rangle_\gamma$, $A'=\langle A',C\rangle_\gamma$, $B=\langle B\rangle_\gamma$ and $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. By \cref{c:quantifier free type tree}(1,2), it suffices to show that, for any $i,j,s<n$ and $b,b'\in B$:
%
%\[\begin{array}{ll} {\mathrm{(a)}}&a_i\meet b\leq a_j\meet b'\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet b\leq a'_j\meet b'\\ {\mathrm{(b)}}& a_i\meet a_j\leq a_s\meet b\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet a'_j\leq a'_s\meet b \\ {\mathrm{(c)}} & a_i\meet b\leq a_j\meet a_s\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet b\leq a'_j\meet a'_s.\end{array}\]

Thus, it suffices to show that, for any $i,j<n$ and $b,b'\in B$:
%\[\begin{array}{llll}{\mathrm{(a)}}&b\leq a_i\Leftrightarrow b\leq a_i' & {\mathrm{(b)}}& a_i\leq b\Leftrightarrow a'_i\leq b \\ {\mathrm{(c)}}& a_i\meet b=a_j\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet b=a'_j & {\mathrm{(d)}}& a_i\meet b=b'\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet b=b'.\end{array}\]

%\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\alph*)}}]
%\item By symmetry, it suffices to show that $b\leq a_i$ implies $b\leq a'_i$. Suppose $b\leq a_i$; since $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, there is $c\in C$ such that $b\leq c\leq a_i$. Since $\qftp_{\gamma}(A/C)=\qftp_{\gamma}(A'/C)$, we conclude that $c\leq a'_i$, so $b\leq a'_i$. 
%\item By symmetry, it suffices to show that $a_i\leq b$ implies $a'_i\leq b$. Suppose $a_i\leq b$ and, aiming a contradiction, assume $a_i'\not\leq b$. Consider $b'\coloneqq b\meet \gamma\in B$. Since $a_i,b'\leq b$, we have that $a_i\leq b'$ or $b'\leq a_i\leq b$. 
%
%In the first case, $b'\in B$ and $a_i\leq b'$ with $a_i,b'\leq \gamma$. Since $\qftp_\gamma(A/C)=\qftp_\gamma(A'/C)$, we get that $a'_i\leq \gamma$ too. As we assume $a'_i\not\leq b$, we conclude that $b'<a'_i$. However, in that case, by point {\rm{(a)}}, we conclude that $b'\leq a_i$, so $b'=a_i$. Then, $b'\in A\cap B=C$. %

\label{l:cone-independence finite} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree and $\gamma$ a point. Then, $\indepe[\gamma]{}$ satisfies the following properties:
\begin{enumerate}[wide]
\item[\rm{(Quantifier free definability)}] For any finite variables $x,y,z$, there is a quantifier free formula $\phi(x,y,z)$ in $\lang_\gamma$ such that, for any tuples $a,b,c$ (on the corresponding variables), $a\indepe[\gamma]{c}b$ if and only if $\vDash \phi(a,b,c)$.
\item[\rm{(Quantifier free invariance)}] If $a\indepe[\gamma]{c}b$, then $a'\indepe[\gamma]{c'}b'$  for any $a',b',c'$ such that $\qftp_{\gamma}(a',b',c')=\qftp_{\gamma}(a,b,c)$. 
\item[\rm{(Embedding invariance)}] If $f:\ T\rightarrow T'$ is an embedding of meet{\hyp}trees, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ if and only if $f(A)\indepe[f(\gamma)]{f(C)}f(B)$.
\item[\rm{(Monotonicity)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $A_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0\subseteq B$, then $A_0\indepe[\gamma]{C}B_0$.
\item[\rm{(Normality)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, then $AC\indepe[\gamma]{C}BC$.
\item[\rm{(Left and right existence)}] $A\indepe[\gamma]{A}B$ and $A\indepe[\gamma]{B}B$.
\item[\rm{(Finite character)}] $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ if and only if $A_0\indepe[\gamma]{C}B_0$ for every $A_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0\subseteq B$ finite.
\item[\rm{(Left and right base monotonicity)}] Suppose $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $C\subseteq D\subseteq A$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$. If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$.
\item[\rm{(Left and right transitivity)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$ and $D\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ with $C\subseteq D\subseteq A$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. If $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$ and $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$ with $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$.
\item[\rm{(Quantifier free stationarity)}] Suppose $a\indepe[\gamma]{C}b$ and $a'\indepe[\gamma]{C}b'$ with $\qftp_{\gamma}(a'/C)$ $=\qftp_{\gamma}(a/C)$ and $\qftp_{\gamma}(b'/C)=\qftp_{\gamma}(b/C)$, then $\qftp_{\gamma}(a'b'/C)=\qftp_{\gamma}(ab/C)$.
\end{enumerate}
\begin{proof} Quantifier free definability is obvious from \cref{d:cone-independence}. Quantifier free invariance and embedding invariance are trivial consequences of quantifier free definability. Monotonicity, normality, existence and finite character are trivial. It remains to check base monotonicity, transitivity and quantifier free stationarity:
\begin{enumerate}[wide]
\item[\rm{(Base monotonicity)}] Let $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$. We want to show that $A\indepe[\gamma]{C'}B$. Note that, by monotonicity, $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$. By  \cref{l:cone-independence and generated}, $\langle AD\rangle_\gamma= \langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cup \langle D\rangle_\gamma$. Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\roman*)}}, wide]
%\item $\langle A,C'\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle B,C'\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle C\rangle_{\gamma}\cup\langle C'\rangle_\gamma=\langle C'\rangle_\gamma$. 
\item Pick $a\in\langle AD\rangle_\gamma=\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cup\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle B\rangle_\gamma$ such that $b\leq a$. If $a\in \langle D\rangle_\gamma$, then $b\leq a\leq a$ with $a\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$. Otherwise, $a\in\langle AC\rangle_\gamma$ and, as $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, there is $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma\subseteq\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ such that $b\leq c\leq a$.
\item Pick $a\in\langle AD\rangle_\gamma=\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cup\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle B\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet b\notin \langle D\rangle_\gamma$. If $a\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$, we get that $a\meet a\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ and $a\meet b\notin \langle D\rangle_\gamma$, so $a\meet a\neq a\meet b$ with $a\in \langle D\rangle_\gamma$. Otherwise, $a\in\langle AC\rangle_\gamma$ and, as $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, there is $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma\subseteq\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet c\neq b\meet c$.
\end{enumerate}

This proves right base monotonicity, and left base monotonicity follows by the symmetric argument.
\item[\rm{(Transitivity)}] Let $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$ and $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$ with $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$. We want to show that $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. 
\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\roman*)}}, wide]
%\item $\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle BC\rangle_\gamma=\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle AD\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle B\rangle_\gamma\subseteq\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle D\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle C\rangle_\gamma$.
\item Pick $a\in\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle AD\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle BD\rangle_\gamma$ with $b\leq a$. As $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$, there is $d\in \langle D\rangle_\gamma$ such that $b\leq d\leq a$. Now, since $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$, there is $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$ such that $d\leq c\leq a$, concluding $b\leq c\leq a$.
\item Pick $a\in\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle AD\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma\subseteq \langle BD\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet c= b\meet c$ for all $c\in \langle C\rangle_\gamma$. Either $a\meet d=b\meet d$ for all $d\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ or there is $d\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$ such that $a\meet d\neq b\meet d$. 

In the first case, as $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$, we conclude $a\meet b\in\langle D\rangle_\gamma$. Now, for any $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$, as $a\meet c=b\meet c$, using \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 2}), we get that $a\meet c=(a\meet c)\meet (b\meet c)=(a\meet b)\meet c$. As $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$, we conclude that $a\meet b=a\meet (a\meet b)\in \langle C\rangle_\gamma$.

In the second case, by \cref{l:four elements}, $a\meet c=d\meet c$ for any $c\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. Therefore, as $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$, we conclude that $a\meet d\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. In particular, $a\meet d=a\meet (a\meet d)=b\meet (a\meet d)=a\meet b\meet d$. Since $a\meet d\neq b\meet d$, by \cref{l:three elements}, we conclude that $a\meet b=a\meet b\meet d=a\meet d\in\langle C\rangle_\gamma$.
\end{enumerate}

This proves right transitivity, and left transitivity follows by the symmetric argument.
\item[\rm{(Quantifier free stationarity)}] By embedding invariance it suffices to consider the case of the universal dense meet{\hyp}tree. By quantifier free invariance, it is enough to show that if $a \indepe[\gamma]{C} B$ and $a'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ with $\qftp(a/C)= \qftp(a'/C)$, then $\qftp_\gamma(a/CB)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/CB)$. It is enough to do the case where $|a|=1$ by monotonicity, base monotonicity and invariance. %Indeed, prove the statement by induction on $|a|$: say $a=a_{<n}a_n$ where $|a_n|=1$ and $n>0$, then, by induction hypothesis and monotonicity, $\qftp(a_{<n}/Cb)=\qftp(a_{<n}'/Cb)$ and, by invariance, we may assume $a_0=a_0'$; but then $a_1'\indepe[\gamma]{Ca_0}b$ by base monotonicity and, by induction hypothesis, $\qftp(a_1/Ca_0b)=\qftp(a_1'/Ca_0b)$.

Without loss of generality, say $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$ and $B=\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. There are two cases: there is $c\in C$ with $a\leq c$ or $e\coloneqq \max\{a\meet c\tq c\in C\}<a$. 

In the first case, take $c\in C$ with $a\leq c$. Since $\qftp(a/C)=\qftp(a'/C)$, $a'\leq c$. By \cref{l:no based cone-independence definition}, $a\indepe[c]{C}B$ and $a'\indepe[c]{C}B$. By point \ref{itm:def cone-independence i} of \cref{d:cone-independence}, $\qftp_{<}(a/\pi(B))=\qftp_{<}(a'/\pi(B))$ where $\pi(B)=\{b\meet c\tq b\in B\}=\{b\in B\tq b\leq c\}$. Then, by \cref{c:semibranch intervals are indiscernible}, $\qftp_\Gamma(a/B)=\qftp_\Gamma(a'/B)$ where $\Gamma$ is the semibranch given by $c$. By \cref{c:quantifier free type tree}(\ref{itm:quantifier free type tree 1},\ref{itm:quantifier free type tree 3}), $\qftp_\gamma(a/B)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/B)$.

In the second case, let $e\coloneqq\max\{a\meet c\tq c\in C\}$ and $e'\coloneqq\max\{a'\meet c\tq c\in C\}$. Since $\qftp_\gamma(a/C)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/C)$, we have $\qftp_\gamma(e/C)=\qftp_\gamma(e'/C)$. Also, by monotonicity, we have $e\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $e'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, but now there is $c\in C$ with $e\leq c$. Thus, by the previous case, $\qftp_\gamma(e/B)=\qftp_\gamma(e'/B)$. By invariance, we may assume $e=e'$. By base monotonicity, $a\indepe[\gamma]{Ce}B$ and $a'\indepe[\gamma]{Ce}B$ with $\qftp_\gamma(a/Ce)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/Ce)$. By \cref{l:no based cone-independence definition}, $a\indepe[e]{Ce}B$ and $a'\indepe[e]{Ce}B$. By choice of $e$, $a,a'\notin \cone_e(C)$. By \cref{l:cones and cone independence}, $a,a'\notin\cone_e(B)$. Hence, by \cref{c:cones are indiscernible}, $\qftp_e(a/B)=\qftp_e(a'/B)$. By \cref{c:quantifier free type tree}(\ref{itm:quantifier free type tree 1}), $\qftp_\gamma(a/B)=\qftp_\gamma(a'/B)$.

Without loss of generality, assume $A=\langle A,C\rangle_\gamma$, $A'=\langle A',C\rangle_\gamma$, $B=\langle B\rangle_\gamma$ and $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. By \cref{c:quantifier free type tree}(1,2), it suffices to show that, for any $i,j,s<n$ and $b,b'\in B$:
%
%\[\begin{array}{ll} {\mathrm{(a)}}&a_i\meet b\leq a_j\meet b'\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet b\leq a'_j\meet b'\\ {\mathrm{(b)}}& a_i\meet a_j\leq a_s\meet b\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet a'_j\leq a'_s\meet b \\ {\mathrm{(c)}} & a_i\meet b\leq a_j\meet a_s\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet b\leq a'_j\meet a'_s.\end{array}\]
%
%Now, for any $i,j<n$, there is $k<n$ such that $a_i\meet a_j=a_k$ and $a'_i\meet a'_j=a'_k$. Also, by \cref{l:cone-independence and generated}, for any $i<n$ and $b\in B$, either $a_i\meet b=a_j$ for some $j<n$ or $a_i\meet b=b'$ for some $b'\in B$. Thus, it suffices to show that, for any $i,j<n$ and $b,b'\in B$:
%\[\begin{array}{llll}{\mathrm{(a)}}&b\leq a_i\Leftrightarrow b\leq a_i' & {\mathrm{(b)}}& a_i\leq b\Leftrightarrow a'_i\leq b \\ {\mathrm{(c)}}& a_i\meet b=a_j\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet b=a'_j & {\mathrm{(d)}}& a_i\meet b=b'\Leftrightarrow a'_i\meet b=b'.\end{array}\]

context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem} \label{l:cone-independence finite} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree and $\gamma$ a point. Then, $\indepe[\gamma]{}$ satisfies the following properties: \begin{enumerate}[wide] \item[\rm{(Quantifier free definability)}] For any finite variables $x,y,z$, there is a quantifier free formula $\phi(x,y,z)$ in $\lang_\gamma$ such that, for any tuples $a,b,c$ (on the corresponding variables), $a\indepe[\gamma]{c}b$ if and only if $\vDash \phi(a,b,c)$. \item[\rm{(Quantifier free invariance)}] If $a\indepe[\gamma]{c}b$, then $a'\indepe[\gamma]{c'}b'$ for any $a',b',c'$ such that $\qftp_{\gamma}(a',b',c')=\qftp_{\gamma}(a,b,c)$. \item[\rm{(Embedding invariance)}] If $f:\ T\rightarrow T'$ is an embedding of meet{\hyp}trees, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ if and only if $f(A)\indepe[f(\gamma)]{f(C)}f(B)$. \item[\rm{(Monotonicity)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $A_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0\subseteq B$, then $A_0\indepe[\gamma]{C}B_0$. \item[\rm{(Normality)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, then $AC\indepe[\gamma]{C}BC$. \item[\rm{(Left and right existence)}] $A\indepe[\gamma]{A}B$ and $A\indepe[\gamma]{B}B$. \item[\rm{(Finite character)}] $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ if and only if $A_0\indepe[\gamma]{C}B_0$ for every $A_0\subseteq A$ and $B_0\subseteq B$ finite. \item[\rm{(Left and right base monotonicity)}] Suppose $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $C\subseteq D\subseteq A$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$. If $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ and $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$. \item[\rm{(Left and right transitivity)}] If $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$ and $D\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ with $C\subseteq D\subseteq A$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. If $A\indepe[\gamma]{D}B$ and $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}D$ with $C\subseteq D\subseteq B$, then $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. \item[\rm{(Quantifier free stationarity)}] Su

\label{l:easy independence} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\gamma$ a point and $A,B,C$ subsets. Assume that, for all $a\in A$ and $b\in B$, we have $a\meet b\leq \gamma$ when $a\geq \gamma$ and $a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$ when $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$. Then, $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$.
\begin{proof} Take $b,b'\in B\cup\{\gamma\}$ and $a\in A$. If $a\geq \gamma$, clearly $a\meet (b\meet b')\leq a\meet b\leq\gamma$. If $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$, $a\meet\gamma<\min\{b\meet \gamma,b'\meet\gamma\}=b\meet b'\meet \gamma$. Therefore, we can assume without loss of generality that $B=\langle B\rangle_\gamma$.  

Note that the condition does not mention $C$. Thus, by transitivity (and induction), we can assume that $A=\{a\}$ is a singleton. Also, we can assume without loss of generality that $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. There are two cases, $a\geq \gamma$ or $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$.
\begin{enumerate}[wide]
\item[Case $a\geq \gamma$.] By existence, we can assume $a>\gamma$. 

First, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence i} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Let $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$, and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $b'\leq a'$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. Thus, $b'\leq a$, $b'\leq b$ and $b'\leq c_a$, so $b'\leq a\meet b\meet c_a\leq \gamma\meet c_a\leq a\meet c_a=a'$ by hypothesis. If $c_a\in C$,  we are done. If $c_a=a$, $\gamma\meet c_a=\gamma\in C$. 

Now, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence ii} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Take $a'\in\langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$ and $b'\in \langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. By hypothesis, $a'\meet b'\leq a\meet b\meet c_a\leq \gamma\meet c_a$. If $a'\meet b'=\gamma\meet c_a$, we are done. Otherwise, $a'\meet (\gamma\meet c_a)=(a\meet c_a)\meet(\gamma\meet c_a)=(a\meet \gamma)\meet c_a=\gamma\meet c_a$ and $b'\meet (\gamma\meet c_a)=b'\meet (a'\meet \gamma \meet c_a)=(a'\meet b')\meet(\gamma\meet c_a)=a'\meet b'<\gamma\meet c_a=a'\meet (\gamma\meet c_a)$.
\item[Case $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$.] \

First, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence i} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Let $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$, and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $b'\leq a'$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. Then, by hypothesis, $\min\{b\meet \gamma,c_b\meet \gamma\}=b'\meet \gamma\leq a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$, so $c_b\meet \gamma<b\meet\gamma$ (in particular, $c_b\in C$). By \cref{l:three elements} applied to $b,c_b,\gamma$, we get $b'=b\meet c_b=c_b\meet \gamma\in C$ and we are done.

Now, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence ii} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Take $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$ and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $a'\meet c=b'\meet c$ for all $c\in C$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. By assumption, $a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$. As $a'\meet\gamma=b'\meet\gamma$, we get $b'\meet \gamma=a'\meet \gamma\leq a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$. In particular, $c_b\neq b$, so $c_b\in C$. By \cref{l:three elements} applied to $b,c_b,\gamma$, we get that $b'\meet \gamma=b\meet c_b=c_b\meet \gamma\in C$. Therefore, $a'\meet b'=a'\meet (b\meet c_b)=a'\meet (b'\meet \gamma)=(a'\meet \gamma)\meet (b'\meet \gamma)=b'\meet \gamma=c_b\meet \gamma\in C$.\qedhere
\end{enumerate}
%
%We need to show that the three conditions of the definition of $\indepe[\gamma]{}$ hold:
%\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\roman*)}}]
%\item Take $x\in \langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. By \cref{l:generated}(2), there are $a\in A$, $b\in B$ and $c,c'\in C$ such that
%\[\begin{array}{llcll} \mathrm{(a)}& x=a=b,&\mathrm{or}& \mathrm{(b)}& x=a\meet c=b,\mathrm{\ or}\\ \mathrm{(c)}& x=a=b\meet c,&\mathrm{or}& \mathrm{(d)}& x=a\meet c=b\meet c'.\end{array}\]
%In cases $\mathrm{(b),(c)}$, it follows that $x=x\meet c=a\meet c=b\meet c$. In case $\mathrm{(d)}$, it follows that $x=x\meet c''=a\meet c''=b\meet c''$ with $c''=c\meet c'$. Thus, there are $a\in A$, $b\in B$ and $c\in C$ such that 
%\[\begin{array}{llcll} \mathrm{(a)}& x=a=b&\mathrm{or}& \mathrm{(b)}& x=a\meet c=b\meet c.\end{array}\]
%\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\alph*)}}]
%\item By assumption, as $a\meet\gamma=x\meet \gamma=b\meet \gamma$, we get that $a,b\geq \gamma$. Thus, by assumption, $x=x\meet x=a\meet b=\gamma\in C$.
%\item Either $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$ or $a\geq \gamma$. 
%
%If $a\meet\gamma<\gamma$, then $a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$ while $a\meet c=b\meet c$, concluding $x=a\meet c=\gamma\meet c\in C$ by \cref{l:four elements}. 
%

context_pack.original_latex_theorem:
\label{l:easy independence} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\gamma$ a point and $A,B,C$ subsets. Assume that, for all $a\in A$ and $b\in B$, we have $a\meet b\leq \gamma$ when $a\geq \gamma$ and $a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$ when $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$. Then, $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$.
\begin{proof} Take $b,b'\in B\cup\{\gamma\}$ and $a\in A$. If $a\geq \gamma$, clearly $a\meet (b\meet b')\leq a\meet b\leq\gamma$. If $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$, $a\meet\gamma<\min\{b\meet \gamma,b'\meet\gamma\}=b\meet b'\meet \gamma$. Therefore, we can assume without loss of generality that $B=\langle B\rangle_\gamma$.  

Note that the condition does not mention $C$. Thus, by transitivity (and induction), we can assume that $A=\{a\}$ is a singleton. Also, we can assume without loss of generality that $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. There are two cases, $a\geq \gamma$ or $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$.
\begin{enumerate}[wide]
\item[Case $a\geq \gamma$.] By existence, we can assume $a>\gamma$. 

First, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence i} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Let $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$, and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $b'\leq a'$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. Thus, $b'\leq a$, $b'\leq b$ and $b'\leq c_a$, so $b'\leq a\meet b\meet c_a\leq \gamma\meet c_a\leq a\meet c_a=a'$ by hypothesis. If $c_a\in C$,  we are done. If $c_a=a$, $\gamma\meet c_a=\gamma\in C$. 

Now, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence ii} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Take $a'\in\langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$ and $b'\in \langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. By hypothesis, $a'\meet b'\leq a\meet b\meet c_a\leq \gamma\meet c_a$. If $a'\meet b'=\gamma\meet c_a$, we are done. Otherwise, $a'\meet (\gamma\meet c_a)=(a\meet c_a)\meet(\gamma\meet c_a)=(a\meet \gamma)\meet c_a=\gamma\meet c_a$ and $b'\meet (\gamma\meet c_a)=b'\meet (a'\meet \gamma \meet c_a)=(a'\meet b')\meet(\gamma\meet c_a)=a'\meet b'<\gamma\meet c_a=a'\meet (\gamma\meet c_a)$.
\item[Case $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$.] \

First, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence i} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Let $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$, and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $b'\leq a'$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. Then, by hypothesis, $\min\{b\meet \gamma,c_b\meet \gamma\}=b'\meet \gamma\leq a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$, so $c_b\meet \gamma<b\meet\gamma$ (in particular, $c_b\in C$). By \cref{l:three elements} applied to $b,c_b,\gamma$, we get $b'=b\meet c_b=c_b\meet \gamma\in C$ and we are done.

Now, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence ii} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Take $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$ and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $a'\meet c=b'\meet c$ for all $c\in C$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. By assumption, $a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$. As $a'\meet\gamma=b'\meet\gamma$, we get $b'\meet \gamma=a'\meet \gamma\leq a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$. In particular, $c_b\neq b$, so $c_b\in C$. By \cref{l:three elements} applied to $b,c_b,\gamma$, we get that $b'\meet \gamma=b\meet c_b=c_b\meet \gamma\in C$. Therefore, $a'\meet b'=a'\meet (b\meet c_b)=a'\meet (b'\meet \gamma)=(a'\meet \gamma)\meet (b'\meet \gamma)=b'\meet \gamma=c_b\meet \gamma\in C$.\qedhere
\end{enumerate}
%

%\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\roman*)}}]
%\item Take $x\in \langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. By \cref{l:generated}(2), there are $a\in A$, $b\in B$ and $c,c'\in C$ such that
%\[\begin{array}{llcll} \mathrm{(a)}& x=a=b,&\mathrm{or}& \mathrm{(b)}& x=a\meet c=b,\mathrm{\ or}\\ \mathrm{(c)}& x=a=b\meet c,&\mathrm{or}& \mathrm{(d)}& x=a\meet c=b\meet c'.\end{array}\]
%In cases $\mathrm{(b),(c)}$, it follows that $x=x\meet c=a\meet c=b\meet c$. In case $\mathrm{(d)}$, it follows that $x=x\meet c''=a\meet c''=b\meet c''$ with $c''=c\meet c'$. Thus, there are $a\in A$, $b\in B$ and $c\in C$ such that 
%\[\begin{array}{llcll} \mathrm{(a)}& x=a=b&\mathrm{or}& \mathrm{(b)}& x=a\meet c=b\meet c.\end{array}\]
%\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{(\alph*)}}]
%\item By assumption, as $a\meet\gamma=x\meet \gamma=b\meet \gamma$, we get that $a,b\geq \gamma$. Thus, by assumption, $x=x\meet x=a\meet b=\gamma\in C$.
%\item Either $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$ or $a\geq \gamma$. 
%
%If $a\meet\gamma<\gamma$, then $a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$ while $a\meet c=b\meet c$, concluding $x=a\meet c=\gamma\meet c\in C$ by \cref{l:four elements}. 
%
%If $a\geq \gamma$, as $x,\gamma\leq a$, either $x\geq \gamma$ or $x<\gamma$. If $x\geq \gamma$, then $b\geq \gamma$ too, so $x=x\meet x=a\meet b\meet c=\gamma\meet c\in C$ by assumption. If $x<\gamma$, then $x=x\meet \gamma=a\meet \gamma\meet c=\gamma\meet c\in C$.
%
%Hence, this shows that $\langle AC\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle BC\rangle_\gamma=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$.
%\end{enumerate}
%\item Take $a\in \langle AC\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $b\leq a$. If $a\in C$ or $b\in C$, there is nothing to prove. For $a,b\notin C$, by \cref{l:generated}(1,3), we know that $a=a_0\meet c$ and $b=b_0\meet c'$  with $a_0\in A$, $b_0\in B$ and $c\in C\cup\{a_0\}$ and $c'\in C\cup\{b_0\}$. Thus, $b\leq a_0$ with $a_0\in A$. Either $a_0\meet \gamma<\gamma$ or $a_0\geq\gamma$. 
%
%In the first case, by assumption, $b\meet \gamma=\min\{b_0\meet \gamma,c'\meet \gamma\}\leq a_0\meet \gamma<b_0\meet \gamma$, so $ c'\meet \gamma<b_0\meet\gamma$ (in particular, $c'\in C$). By \cref{l:three elements}, we get $b=b\meet\gamma=c'\meet \gamma\in C$. Therefore, $b\leq c'\meet \gamma\leq a_0$. If $c=a_0$, then we conclude $b\leq c'\meet \gamma\leq a$ with $c'\meet \gamma\in C$. Otherwise, $b\leq c\meet c'\meet\gamma\leq a$ with $c\meet c'\meet \gamma\in C$. 
%
%In the second case, as $b,\gamma\leq a_0$, we get that $b< \gamma$ or $b\geq \gamma$. In the former case, we get that $b< \gamma\leq a_0$. Thus, if $c=a_0$, we already conclude that $b< \gamma\leq a$ with $\gamma\in C$. Otherwise, $b\leq \gamma\meet c\leq a$ with $\gamma\meet c\in C$. In the later case, $b\geq \gamma$, so $b_0\geq \gamma$ too. Thus, $a_0\meet b_0=\gamma$ by assumption. If $c'=b_0$, we conclude that $b=b\meet a_0=b_0\meet a_0=\gamma\leq a_0\leq a$ with $\gamma\in C$. Otherwise, $b=b\meet a_0=\gamma\meet c'\leq a$ with $\gamma\meet c'\in C$.
%\item Take $a\in \langle AC\rangle_\gamma$ and $b\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $a\meet \gamma=b\meet \gamma$. If $a,b\notin C$, by \cref{l:generated}(1,3), we know that $a=a_0\meet c$ and $b=b_0\meet c'$  with $a_0\in A$, $b_0\in B$ and $c\in C\cup\{a_0\}$ and $c'\in C\cup\{b_0\}$. Set $c''=c$ if $c'=b_0$, $c''=c'$ if $c=a_0$ and $c''=c\meet c'$ if $c\neq a_0$, $c'\neq b_0$. Either $a_0\meet\gamma<\gamma$ or $a_0\geq \gamma$. 
%
%In the first case, by assumption, $a_0\meet \gamma<b_0\meet \gamma$. As $a\meet\gamma=b\meet\gamma$, in particular $c'\neq b_0$. We get then $a_0\meet (c''\meet \gamma)=b_0\meet (c''\meet \gamma)$. By \cref{l:four elements}, we get $a_0\meet (c''\meet \gamma)=\gamma\meet (c''\meet\gamma)=c''\meet \gamma$. On the other hand, by \cref{l:three elements}, $a_0\meet b_0=a_0\meet b_0\meet \gamma$. Therefore, $a\meet b=(a_0\meet b_0)\meet c''=(a_0\meet b_0\meet c'')\meet \gamma=(a_0\meet (c''\meet \gamma))\meet (b_0\meet (c''\meet \gamma))=c''\meet \gamma\in C$.

 %

\end{proof}
context_pack.exact_source_excerpt: \begin{lem}\label{l:easy independence} Let $T$ be a meet{\hyp}tree, $\gamma$ a point and $A,B,C$ subsets. Assume that, for all $a\in A$ and $b\in B$, we have $a\meet b\leq \gamma$ when $a\geq \gamma$ and $a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$ when $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$. Then, $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. \begin{proof} Take $b,b'\in B\cup\{\gamma\}$ and $a\in A$. If $a\geq \gamma$, clearly $a\meet (b\meet b')\leq a\meet b\leq\gamma$. If $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$, $a\meet\gamma<\min\{b\meet \gamma,b'\meet\gamma\}=b\meet b'\meet \gamma$. Therefore, we can assume without loss of generality that $B=\langle B\rangle_\gamma$. Note that the condition does not mention $C$. Thus, by transitivity (and induction), we can assume that $A=\{a\}$ is a singleton. Also, we can assume without loss of generality that $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$. There are two cases, $a\geq \gamma$ or $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$. \begin{enumerate}[wide] \item[Case $a\geq \gamma$.] By existence, we can assume $a>\gamma$. First, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence i} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Let $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$, and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $b'\leq a'$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 3}), we know that $a'=a\meet c_a$ and $b'=b\meet c_b$ with $b\in B$ and $c_a\in C\cup\{a\}$ and $c_b\in C\cup\{b\}$. Thus, $b'\leq a$, $b'\leq b$ and $b'\leq c_a$, so $b'\leq a\meet b\meet c_a\leq \gamma\meet c_a\leq a\meet c_a=a'$ by hypothesis. If $c_a\in C$, we are done. If $c_a=a$, $\gamma\meet c_a=\gamma\in C$. Now, we prove condition \ref{itm:def cone-independence ii} of \cref{d:cone-independence}. Take $a'\in\langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$ and $b'\in \langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. If $a'\in C$ or $b'\in C$, there is nothing to prove. By

Assume that, for all $a\in A$ and $b\in B$, we have $a\meet b\leq \gamma$ when $a\geq \gamma$ and $a\meet \gamma<b\meet \gamma$ when $a\meet \gamma<\gamma$.
Therefore, we can assume without loss of generality that $B=\langle B\rangle_\gamma$.
Thus, by transitivity (and induction), we can assume that $A=\{a\}$ is a singleton.
Also, we can assume without loss of generality that $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$.
\begin{enumerate}[wide] \item[Case $a\geq \gamma$.] By existence, we can assume $a>\gamma$.
Let $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$, and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $b'\leq a'$.
Let $a'\in \langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma$, and $b'\in\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$ with $b'\leq a'$.

semantic_equivalence_artifact.original_latex_theorem:
\label{l:cone-independence} $\indepe[\gamma]{}$ in $\Tt_\gamma$ is a stationary weak independence relation satisfying reflexivity and the independent generation property.
\begin{proof} Reflexivity follows from \cref{o:reflexivity}. By \cref{l:cone-independence and generated}, we already know that $\indepe[\gamma]{}$ satisfies the independent generation property. By quantifier elimination and \cref{l:cone-independence finite}, we already have invariance, monotonicity, normality, existence, base monotonicity, transitivity and stationarity. It remains to show extension. By \cref{o:left extension implies right extension}, it suffices to show left extension:
\begin{claim} \label{cl:claim extension} Let $C$ and $B$ be finite subset with $C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$ and $B=\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. Let $a$ be a single element such that $\langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma=\langle C\rangle_\gamma\cup\{a\}$. Then, there is $a'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ with $a'\equiv^\gamma_Ca$.
\begin{proof} The case where $a\in C$ is trivial, so assume $a\notin C$. Write $C_{<a}\coloneqq\{c\in C\tq c<a\}$ and $C_{>a}\coloneqq\{c\in C\tq c>a\}$. There are three possible cases:
\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm{Case \arabic*:}}, wide]
\item $C_{<a}=\emptyset$. Since we assume that $\langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma=C\cup\{a\}$, we have that $a\meet c\in C$ or $a\leq c$ for all $c\in C$. Thus, if $C_{<a}=\emptyset$, we get that $a< c$ for all $c\in C$, i.e. $C=C_{>a}$. Take $b_0\coloneqq\bigwedge B$ and $a'<b_0$ arbitrary. Since $B=\langle B\rangle_\gamma$, we have that $a'<b_0\leq \gamma$, so $a'\meet \gamma=a'<b_0\leq b\meet \gamma$ for any $b\in B$. Thus, by \cref{l:easy independence}, we conclude that $a'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. On the other hand, since $C\subseteq B$, we have that $a'<c$ for all $c\in C$. Thus, $\qftp_{<}(a/C)=\qftp_{<}(a'/C)$. As $a'\meet c=a'$ for all $c\in C=\langle C\rangle_\gamma$, we have that $\langle a'\, C\rangle_\gamma=C\cup \{a'\}$ by \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 4}). Thus, we conclude that $a\equiv^\gamma_C a'$ by \cref{l:quantifier free type} and quantifier elimination.
\item $C_{>a}=\emptyset$. Since we assume that $\langle a\, C\rangle_\gamma=C\cup\{a\}$, we have that $e\coloneqq\max\{a\meet c\tq c\in C\}\in C$. Pick any $a'>e$ with $a'\notin \cone_e(B)$, which exists by finiteness of $B$. By \cref{l:easy independence}, $a'\indepe[e]{C}B$. By \cref{l:no based cone-independence definition}, as $\gamma,e\in C$, we conclude that $a'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. On the other hand, since $C\subseteq B$, $a'\notin\cone_e(C)$. Also, by choice of $e$, $a\notin\cone_e(C)$. Therefore, by \cref{c:cones are indiscernible} and quantifier elimination, we conclude that $a\equiv^\gamma_Ca'$.  
\item $C_{<a}\neq \emptyset$ and $C_{>a}\neq\emptyset$. Take $c_0\coloneqq\max C_{<a}$ and $c_1\coloneqq \min C_{>a}=\bigwedge C_{>a}$. Now, $c_0<a<c_1$ and there is no $c\in C$ with $c_0<c<c_1$. Take $B_0\coloneqq\{b\in B\tq c_0<b\leq c_1\}$ and $b_0\coloneqq\min B_0$. Pick any $a'$ with $c_0<a'<b_0$. Obviously, $a'\meet c_1=a'=\max\{a'\meet c\tq c\in C\}$, so $\langle a'\, C\rangle_\gamma=C\cup\{a'\}$ by \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 4}). Since there is no $c$ with $c_0<c<c_1$, we get that $C_{<a}=\{c\in C\tq c<a'\}$ and $C_{>a}=\{c\in C\tq a'<c\}$. Hence, $\qftp_<(a/C)=\qftp_<(a'/C)$, concluding that $a\equiv^\gamma_Ca'$ by \cref{l:quantifier free type} and quantifier elimination. It remains to check that $a'\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$. 
\begin{enumerate}[label={\rm(\roman*)}, wide]
%\item Since $\langle a',C\rangle_\gamma=C\cup \{a'\}$ and $B=\langle B,C\rangle_\gamma$, we get $\langle a',C\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle B,C\rangle=(\{a'\}\cup C)\cap B$. As $c_0<a'<c_1$ and $a'<b_0$, we get that $a'\notin B$, concluding $\langle a',C\rangle_\gamma\cap \langle B,C\rangle=C$.
\item Take $b\in B$ such that $b<a'$. Then, $b<b_0$, so $b\notin B_0$, concluding $b\leq c_0<a'$. 
\item Take $b\in B$. Either $b\meet c_1\leq c_0<a'=a'\meet c_1$ or $a'\meet c_1=a'<b_0\leq b\meet c_1$. In any case, $a'\meet c_1\neq b\meet c_1$ where $c_1\in C$.  \claimqed
\end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}
\end{proof}
\end{claim}

Now, suppose $A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ with $A,B,C$ finite and $a$ an arbitrary point. Without loss of generality, $A=\langle AC\rangle_\gamma$ and $B=\langle BC\rangle_\gamma$. By \cref{l:generated}(\ref{itm:generated 4}), $\langle a\, A\rangle_\gamma=A\cup\{e,a\}$ with $e\coloneqq\max\{a\meet d\tq d\in A\}$; in particular, $\langle e\, A\rangle_\gamma=A\cup\{e\}$. By \cref{cl:claim extension}, there is $e'$ such that $e'\indepe[\gamma]{A}B$ and $e'\equiv^\gamma_A e$. Set $B'\coloneqq\langle B\, e'\rangle_\gamma$. Take $a'$ such that $a'e'\equiv^\gamma_A ae$, so $\langle e'a'A\rangle_\gamma=A\cup \{e',a'\}$. Again, by \cref{cl:claim extension}, there is $a''$ such that $a''\indepe[\gamma]{Ae'}B'$ with $a''\equiv^\gamma_{Ae'}a'$, so $a''\equiv^\gamma_{A} a$. By monotonicity, $a''\indepe[\gamma]{Ae'}B$, by transitivity, $a''e'\indepe[\gamma]{A}B$ and $a''e'A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$, so $a''A\indepe[\gamma]{C}B$ by monotonicity. Hence, we conclude that $\indepe[\gamma]{}$ satisfies left extension.  
\end{proof}
